diagrams.title: Alphabet Definition and Symbols
diagrams.description: A visual representation of an alphabet as a finite set of symbols. Shows the notation Σ = {a, b} with symbols displayed inside a set boundary, demonstrating that an alphabet is simply a non-empty finite set of characters.
diagrams.purpose: Explains that an alphabet is a finite, non-empty set of symbols from which strings are constructed. This is the foundational building block for all formal languages.

diagrams.title: String Formation from Alphabet
diagrams.description: Demonstrates how strings are created by concatenating symbols from the alphabet. Shows examples of strings of increasing length (ε, a, b, aa, ab, ba, bb, aaa) with clear visual grouping showing the progression from length 0 to length 3.
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates that a string is a finite sequence of symbols from an alphabet, and shows how strings of different lengths are formed through concatenation. Introduces the concept of empty string (ε).

diagrams.title: Language as a Set of Strings
diagrams.description: Shows the relationship between alphabet, strings, and language. Uses a Venn-like diagram or set representation where the language L = {a^n | n ≥ 0} is depicted as a collection of valid strings, with visual distinction between valid strings (in the language set) and invalid combinations (outside).
elements: Valid strings in language (ε, a, aa, aaa, aaaa)
Subset notation showing strings belong to language
diagrams.purpose: Explains that a language is a set of strings formed from an alphabet. Demonstrates that languages can be finite or infinite, and shows the membership relationship between strings and languages.
